# Transcrição: 04-My Favorite Deal Type for Newbies

- **Fonte:** 04-My Favorite Deal Type for Newbies
- **Duração:** 05:13
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:04]

My favorite deal type for newbie business acquirers is the intrapreneurship deal. That's where I got started. As you age with experience and you can show that you can create results, access to capital actually starts to become easy so you can start buying more and more businesses. But if you're just starting out and you're targeting a $3-5 million business, this could be difficult. And if you haven't spent any time to study the business, if you have no work experience in the business, if you have no business acumen for operations, finance, marketing, all the elements, running teams of people, it may be very difficult for you for your first time acquisition to buy a $3-5 million business. It's going to be very hard starting out to get financing at businesses that size with no experience. If you have a good background and you're following the approach we're outlining in this course, even on your first time, it's possible to acquire a business though it will take hard work. So if you're just starting out in your career, maybe you don't have the confidence to take down debt or try to buy a business, this is the perfect vehicle for you to create wealth in your life. It just takes some patience. I see long-term business owners that are highly skilled all the time working in businesses where the owner decides to sell, but they're not having a conversation about how they could potentially buy it. If they're not having the conversation, then the owner has nowhere else to go but outside of the person working in the business. The intrapreneur vehicle path to buying a business is the most safest and effective way to do it for most first-time business owners. Working for a business where you can become a future owner is a great opportunity and it's a topic I get asked about all the time, but most people have no idea how to go from employee to owner. Intrapreneur to entrepreneur. Why? First, because people understand the employee mindset but not an acquisition mindset. So if they become an employee, it's not with the stated goal of owning the business some day. Instead, they simply go for a paycheck or they become a superstar inside the business, but they never develop the right relationship or even ask the owner to make a plan with them so they can eventually buy the business one day. But done properly, the best scenario for an intrapreneur is to create value inside the organization along with relationships. Relationships with the business owner, relationships with the bankers, relationships with the team members, relationships with the customers because the more knowledge, awareness, and

## [03:04 - 05:13]

ability to create value in the business, the more your business owner will recognize that you're a great exit strategy for them. Then have the conversation about the intention of one day owning the business should the business owner ever choose to sell. This will prepare you to have the ultimate conversation for creating a letter of intent to eventually buy the business. At that point, with the interest from the seller, you can court other people with funds to invest because you're an example of someone who knows how to run that business. For example, if you get the business owner to agree to sell to you, you can go to investors showing them how much value you've created in the business. Even if they put the money in so you can buy the business, you can do an equity earnings structure. As you overperform, they get paid off, you own equity in the business. You can attract co-investors from existing suppliers or vendors that service the business that you wish to buy. Trust me, many of them don't want their customer to go out of business or disappear. They will typically entertain co-investing in an acquisition rather than risking losing the contract entirely during a sale to a third party. We'll cover this later, but you'll need to demonstrate to those stakeholders that you have what I call the operational lid, the ability to grow the value in the business and show the sophistication of understanding and explaining how you're going to do that. Note that it's often easier to raise capital from small business loans and or personal loans from friends and family, but the amount you need might be limited based on the size. It comes with significant risk because if your family or friends aren't made whole, damage or destroy potentially close relationships forever. Next, we'll take a look at the most common deal types I see. It will give you an even more perspective on the world of business acquisitions. I'll see you in the next lesson.
